sourceWorkbook: /Users/ralfheeblakebarrios/Downloads/LGU Schedules and Profiles for PLDI KII (1).xlsx

profiles.name: Zamboanga City
profiles.background: Master Mariner, Councilor from 2019 to 2022 and Representative from 2022 to 2025. Filipino father and Cambodian mother.
profiles.vulnerabilityRationale:
On Domain A, the strongest drivers appear to be strategic proximity and direct foreign donations, with some contribution from foreign-linked receipts. The city’s location, maritime importance, and proximity to strategic corridors likely explain why the model treats it as structurally exposed. This does not mean malign influence exists; it means the city sits in a space where foreign engagement can matter more. For interviews, the practical question is whether foreign interactions come mainly through logistics, maritime security relevance, humanitarian assistance, port-linked activity, or subnational diplomacy.

On Domain B, the score is high because of institutional opacity, civic space/media-civil society closure, and party alignment/dynastic concentration indicators. That does not prove governance weakness. It means the model sees conditions where scrutiny, contestation, or transparency may be more limited than in many other LGUs. Interviewers should ask how decisions on donations, international engagements, port-related activity, or sister-city links are documented and reviewed.

On Domain C, the strongest driver is current foreign presence, with some contribution from the incident/report signal. This suggests the city is not just structurally exposed on paper; there are present-day patterns worth asking about. The interview angle is to identify whether this presence is diplomatic, commercial, humanitarian, security-adjacent, education-related, or something else.
profiles.engagementAnalysis: Zamboanga City functions as a primary commercial and logistical gateway to the Zamboanga Peninsula and the broader Brunei Darussalam-Indonesia-Malaysia-Philippines East ASEAN Growth Area (BIMP-EAGA). Foreign engagements in this jurisdiction are structured around agro-industrial export development, climate resilience, and establishing regulatory frameworks for international trade.Foreign capital from East Asia and the Middle East views the Zamboanga City Special Economic Zone Authority (ZAMBOECOZONE) as a strategic node for specialized industries, particularly the global Halal market. The LGU has actively courted investors from Singapore, the United Arab Emirates (UAE), and Saudi Arabia to participate in a 100-hectare property project designed exclusively for Halal processing and manufacturing. By aligning local agro-industrial output with Middle Eastern and Singaporean consumption standards, the LGU structurally integrates itself into Islamic finance and food security supply chains. Technical validation for such industrial zones is provided by advanced Asian economies; for instance, Singapore-based SA Geolab International Pte Ltd established a geotechnical soil testing facility in the city in December 2023, providing the engineering benchmarks utilized to attract heavier foreign infrastructure.Simultaneously, the city is a focal point for Western and East Asian developmental aid targeting governance and climate vulnerabilities. In May 2023, the United States Agency for International Development (USAID) and the Korea International Cooperation Agency (KOICA) executed a joint ₱111.5 million grant partnership to enhance the climate resilience of six Philippine cities, with Zamboanga City serving as a primary beneficiary. This collaborative ODA model demonstrates how traditional bilateral donors pool sub-national capital to disseminate climate adaptation technology and increase local access to climate financing. European actors have also engaged directly with the municipality; the Spanish Red Cross funded a ₱38-million "Green Livelihood Project," delivering capacity truck scales and financial aid to coastal villages in Zamboanga City in March 2024 to build a resilient circular waste economy.Foreign PartnerProject / EngagementSectorValue / StatusSpain (Red Cross)Green Livelihood ProjectEnvironmental / Waste₱38 Million South Korea / USClimate Resilient Cities ProjectClimate AdaptationPart of ₱111.5M Grant SingaporeSA Geolab Geotechnical FacilityIndustrial InfrastructureOperational (Dec 2023) United StatesCities for Enhanced GovernanceCivil Society / GovernanceMOU Signed (Jun 2023)

profiles.name: Puerto Princesa City
profiles.age: ~69–70 (birth year widely reported as 1956, exact date not consistently published)
profiles.background: A veteran official known for his 'Apuradong Serbisyo' brand. His 2025–2028 term focuses on transforming Puerto Princesa into a major cruise ship hub and strengthening disaster resilience through partnerships with the U.S. Navy.
profiles.vulnerabilityRationale:
On Domain A, the main drivers are low local revenue self-reliance inverse and strategic proximity. The city does not appear to score high on paradiplomacy or foreign donations, so this is less about overt foreign outreach and more about underlying structural vulnerability plus geography. In plain terms, the model may be saying that the city’s fiscal structure and geostrategic setting make it worth examining even without a large number of visible foreign-linked transactions.

On Domain B, the strongest driver is institutional opacity, with additional contribution from party alignment. This suggests the case is less about civic closure and more about how decisions and controls are organized. Researchers should ask how infrastructure, tourism, maritime, or cruise-related partnerships are processed and disclosed.

On Domain C, the main signal is current foreign presence, rather than narrative divergence or incident-heavy reporting. That can mean a live foreign footprint is present in the local context, but not necessarily in a contentious way. The interviews should distinguish whether this is linked to tourism, maritime activity, education, development cooperation, retirees, or other formal presence.
profiles.engagementAnalysis: Puerto Princesa City, situated in the province of Palawan, operates at the nexus of environmental preservation, tourism, and maritime domain awareness. Foreign engagements here are frequently characterized by ecological and utility initiatives that align with the strategic and economic objectives of external powers.South Korean capital is establishing a significant footprint in the city's real estate and utility sectors. In late 2023, executives from KDS Asset Management, a South Korean firm, engaged the city government to formalize investments in the Sta. Lucia Environmental Estate. This signals a shift from traditional tourism—bolstered by a 2019 sister-city pact with Seogwipo City, South Korea —toward hard infrastructure and environmental zoning. Furthermore, the city's logistical capacity continues to rely on legacy South Korean state funding, notably the $71.6 million ODA loan from the Export-Import Bank of Korea (KEXIM) that financed the Puerto Princesa International Airport expansion. Furthermore, in 2025, the Philippine Economic Zone Authority (PEZA) approved the Palawan Mega Ecozone, which includes 4,000 hectares located in Barangays Montible and Sta. Lucia within Puerto Princesa City, designed to attract agro-industrial and marine biotechnology investments primarily from South Korea, China, and Japan.The European Union and the United States have actively utilized the city for capacity-building programs. In April 2024, the EU’s CRIMARIO program conducted the ALALAYAN multilateral maritime exercise off the coast of Puerto Princesa, integrating 22 Philippine agencies into the European-designed Indo-Pacific Information Sharing Platform (IORIS). Concurrently, USAID implements the "Fish Right" and "Safe Water" programs, which provided municipal hydrological assistance and ₱64-million in conservation grants to local civil society organizations in March 2023 to support coastal habitats and local fisherfolk.

profiles.name: Iloilo City
profiles.background: Daughter of former Mayor Jerry Treñas, she transitioned from executive assistant to mayor in 2025. Her 'Iloilo Next' vision prioritizes digital governance, social services, and maintaining the city’s status as a top business hub.
profiles.vulnerabilityRationale:
On Domain A, the key drivers are paradiplomacy intensity, strategic proximity, and a small but non-zero economic enclave score. This suggests the city likely has multiple foreign-facing channels rather than one dominant source. The paradiplomacy score is one of the clearer hints that sister-city relations, foreign partner cities, or multiple international linkages may be part of the explanation. Researchers should ask what these partnerships actually involve: investment promotion, cultural exchange, urban development, digital governance, climate, or trade.

On Domain B, the largest drivers are institutional opacity, civic space/media-civil society, and dynastic concentration. This does not automatically mean poor governance. It means the city’s governance profile, taken comparatively, gives the model reason to probe how contestation, disclosure, and elite concentration intersect with foreign-facing engagements.

On Domain C, the city has non-zero values across narrative alignment, current foreign presence, and reports, but none looks overwhelmingly dominant. That suggests a broad but moderate signal environment. Interviews should test whether the city has recurring foreign engagement across trade, consular, education, or investment channels rather than a single defining foreign actor.
profiles.engagementAnalysis: Iloilo City has leveraged its administrative frameworks to attract foreign development assistance and host multilateral economic integration summits. The LGU operates as an autonomous diplomatic hub, interfacing directly with foreign states to secure urban development projects.The city's diplomatic capacity was demonstrated when it hosted the 49th High-Level Task Force on ASEAN Economic Integration (HLTF-EI) in February 2026. This event gathered high-ranking economic officials from ASEAN Member States, alongside external partners such as China, Japan, and South Korea, providing the LGU with a direct platform to promote the newly modernized ₱2.35-billion Iloilo Commercial Port Complex to foreign investors. The local government systematically utilized its heritage sites and UNESCO Creative City status to facilitate business matching sessions during the summit.Foreign missions actively seek out the LGU for bilateral cooperation. South Korea has been particularly active; following the signing of a sister-city agreement with Dobong-gu, Seoul in March 2024 for tourism and economic exchange , the South Korean government, via KOICA, included Iloilo City in its joint ₱111.5 million climate resilience funding initiative with USAID to develop climate adaptation technology. Simultaneously, the PRC engages the city through diplomatic channels; China's Consul General utilized sub-national visits in late 2023 and 2025 to push for the deepening of Iloilo's existing partnerships with Chinese industrial hubs like Qingdao, Guangzhou, and Wuhan. Furthermore, in 2025, the city hosted delegations from Singapore and Sweden to discuss educational partnerships with the National University of Singapore (NUS) and broader European trade ties.

profiles.name: Nueva Ecija
profiles.background: A long-dominant political figure in Central Luzon. Despite his 2025 re-election, he faced a one-year suspension by the Ombudsman in late 2025 due to administrative issues regarding quarry permits.
profiles.vulnerabilityRationale:
On Domain A, the most substantial contributors are low local revenue self-reliance inverse and strategic proximity. There is little sign here of paradiplomacy, donations, or enclave-driven exposure. So the interpretation is not “foreign engagement is visibly dense,” but rather “the province may be structurally exposed if foreign-linked opportunities or pressures do emerge.”

On Domain B, the profile is strong and looks central to why this province was included: institutional opacity, party alignment, and absence of FOI ordinance all contribute. That makes this a useful case for asking how approvals, aid, donations, and external engagements are managed internally.

On Domain C, current foreign presence and reports both contribute. That is enough to push it into the C-flag category even without an obvious narrative component. Interviewers should ask whether the relevant signals are tied to agriculture, infrastructure, logistics corridors, training partnerships, or particular foreign-linked projects.
profiles.engagementAnalysis: Nueva Ecija operates as the agricultural center of Central Luzon while also hosting Fort Magsaysay, a major military reservation and Enhanced Defense Cooperation Agreement (EDCA) site. Foreign engagements in this province involve a combination of military logistics, high-value agricultural technology, and renewable energy investments.South Korea and Japan execute massive geoeconomic interventions in the province's civilian sector. South Korea, through KOICA, financed the $100-million Korea Agricultural Machinery Industry Complex (KAMIC) in Cabanatuan City, which commenced construction in late 2023. This 20-hectare facility serves as the country's first large-scale agricultural machinery design and prototyping center, integrating South Korean engineering standards into local agricultural mechanization. Japan utilizes ODA to underwrite the province's foundational infrastructure. The Japan International Cooperation Agency (JICA) funded the repair of the Talavera River Super Diversion Canal Siphon, officially commissioned in February 2026, which restored irrigation to 39,748 hectares of farmland. The Japanese government also finances solar-powered irrigation pilot projects in the municipality of Llanera to boost rice productivity.European capital is actively reshaping the local energy grid. In 2026, the Philippine Board of Investments granted "green lane" status to a ₱30.92-billion onshore wind power project in Nueva Ecija, backed by Copenhagen Infrastructure Partners, a Danish investment firm. In the defense sector, the United States focuses on disaster preparedness and logistics, executing a donation of relief equipment directly to the Office of Civil Defense depot located within Fort Magsaysay in September 2024. Academic paradiplomacy is also present, with the Nueva Ecija University of Science and Technology (NEUST) signing international cooperation agreements with Indonesia’s Universitas Negeri Semarang in 2025.

profiles.name: Pampanga
profiles.term: 1st term [returned as governor (served as governor 2010–2019; vice governor 2022–2025; governor again 2025–2028)]
profiles.background: The matriarch of the Pineda political family. Her 2025 term emphasizes 'Health is Wealth,' focusing on the province's network of public hospitals and climate change mitigation in the flood-prone Central Luzon basin.
profiles.vulnerabilityRationale:
On Domain A, the big drivers are economic enclaves, low local revenue self-reliance inverse, strategic proximity, and a non-zero direct foreign donations score. This is one of the clearer cases where foreign-facing exposure may be linked to industrial or zone-based activity, logistics, and strategic location rather than sister-city diplomacy. For interviews, it would be useful to ask how foreign-linked firms, locators, or development partners interact with the province.

On Domain B, the score is strong because of institutional opacity, civic space/media-civil society, party alignment, and absence of FOI ordinance. The model seems to be saying that if external influence were to operate, this provincial context may offer channels that deserve scrutiny.

On Domain C, the score is not high enough for a flag. That matters. It suggests that even if the province is structurally exposed, the model does not yet see correspondingly strong live signals. This is a good interview case precisely because it can test the gap between vulnerability and actual manifestation.
profiles.engagementAnalysis: Pampanga functions as an industrial and logistical hub, anchored by the Clark Freeport Zone. The sub-national diplomacy in this LGU is defined by direct capital investments from Chinese, Japanese, and South Korean manufacturing and real estate conglomerates.Chinese commercial entities have established a significant physical presence in the province. In March 2023, the Chinese-backed Global Construct City wholesale market was launched in Mexico, Pampanga. Supported by delegations from Jinjiang City, China, this facility serves as a major distribution hub for construction materials. Concurrently, Japanese capital leverages Pampanga's connectivity for food security operations. A consortium of Japanese firms, led by Nisshin Food Material Company Ltd, identified the Clark Freeport Zone as a primary target for a $500-million aquacultural processing initiative, aiming to farm and package seafood locally for export back to the Japanese domestic market.South Korean investors maintain a robust presence in the highly urbanized city of Angeles, focusing on commercial real estate, consumer retail, and specialized manufacturing. The Philippine Economic Zone Authority (PEZA) reported South Korea as a top source of FDI, driven heavily by projects such as Samsung Electro-Mechanics' (SEMPHIL) ₱51-billion expansion in economic zones within the region. To align the local labor pool with the technical requirements of incoming foreign manufacturing, the United States, through USAID, launched the UPSKILL program in Floridablanca in November 2023, focusing on advanced workforce development.

profiles.name: Baguio City
profiles.background: A retired PNP General and FBI Academy graduate. Known for 'Good Governance' and anti-corruption, his current term focuses on 'Smart City' infrastructure and vocal advocacy for Philippine sovereignty in the West Philippine Sea.
profiles.vulnerabilityRationale:
On Domain A, the standout factor is paradiplomacy intensity, with smaller contributions from foreign-linked receipts, low local revenue self-reliance inverse, and economic enclaves. This is one of the strongest hints in the sheet that multiple foreign partnerships or city-to-city linkages may matter. If the team wants to understand how foreign engagement works through soft channels rather than overt aid or donations, Baguio is a useful case.

On Domain B, the score is high due to institutional opacity, some civic space/media-civil society concern, and absence of FOI ordinance. The B score is enough to flag the city even though dynastic concentration is not especially high relative to others.

On Domain C, the signal score does not cross the threshold. That means the city is not being selected because of strong foreign incident patterns or strong current foreign presence. The value of this case is that it may illustrate high outward connectivity without a correspondingly strong signal environment.
profiles.engagementAnalysis: Baguio City's foreign engagements focus on smart-city digitalization, high-altitude agriculture, and international tourism integration facilitated by an extensive network of sister-city agreements. The LGU actively utilizes municipal-level diplomacy to establish direct international partnerships.Baguio maintains highly active sister-city and friendship pacts with Taebaek (South Korea), Hangzhou and Chongzuo (China), and Hanyu (Japan). The March 2023 Memorandum of Understanding with Taebaek City, signed alongside three major northern Philippine universities, targets joint educational research and institutional capacity building, demonstrating how the LGU leverages foreign municipal ties to elevate its local academic sector. Similarly, exploratory talks with Chongzuo, China, initiated via the Philippine-Guangxi Commercial Association, focus on transferring Chinese urban planning and highland ecotourism models to the Cordillera region. During major events like the Panagbenga Festival in 2026, the city records massive influxes of tourists from its South Korean and Chinese sister cities, heavily driving the local retail economy.To modernize its commercial infrastructure and manage this tourism volume, Baguio City engages in direct corporate partnerships. In November 2024, the LGU signed a five-year agreement with the multinational financial corporation Visa and Thames International. This arrangement provides the city government with "Visa Destination Insights" to track spending patterns while digitizing local micro, small, and medium enterprises (MSMEs).

profiles.name: Lapu-Lapu City
profiles.age: ~52–54 (exact birthdate not consistently published; early 1970s estimate based on profiles)
profiles.background: Formerly the city's lone district representative, she succeeded her husband as mayor in 2025. She is currently focused on hosting the 2026 ASEAN Summit and expanding tourism infrastructure on Mactan Island.
profiles.vulnerabilityRationale:
On Domain A, the major contributors are foreign-linked receipts, low local revenue self-reliance inverse, and strategic proximity, with small paradiplomacy and enclave values. This suggests the city’s exposure may be tied to tourism, airport-linked or Mactan-linked external flows, investment, and broader foreign-facing local economic structure. It is less a sister-city case and more a local economy case.

On Domain B, the drivers are institutional opacity, party alignment/FOI-related factors, while civic space and dynastic concentration are lower. This indicates the B flag is not being driven by a single political-family story but by institutional and formal governance conditions.

On Domain C, there is some signal activity, especially current foreign presence, but not enough to cross the threshold. This again makes it useful for interviews: the city may have substantial external exposure without strong negative-signal manifestation.
profiles.engagementAnalysis: Situated on Mactan Island, Lapu-Lapu City is a primary center for export-oriented manufacturing, aviation, and tourism in the Visayas. The LGU's foreign interactions are predominantly shaped by the Mactan Economic Zone, which serves as a magnet for East Asian technology hardware manufacturing and multinational corporate investments.Current supply chain realignments are introducing new foreign actors to the city. Taiwanese companies, facing land constraints domestically and geopolitical pressure to diversify export bases, are increasingly evaluating zones like Lapu-Lapu City for semiconductor peripherals and electronics manufacturing. Furthermore, Japanese investment in the city is diversifying from pure manufacturing into the wellness and healthcare sectors. Nambu Co., Ltd., a Japanese firm, initiated plans to establish a specialized wellness facility in Lapu-Lapu City, leveraging the Corporate Recovery and Tax Incentives for Enterprises Maximize Opportunities for Reinvigorating the Economy (CREATE MORE) Act to train Filipino caregivers to Japanese healthcare standards.Lapu-Lapu City is also a target for non-traditional diplomatic overtures. In October 2025, Ukrainian Ambassador Yuliia Fediv visited the LGU to negotiate the reestablishment of trade and sister-city ties. By pursuing shared priorities such as smart-city development in a major Philippine economic hub, Ukraine actively courts sub-national diplomatic solidarity and economic linkages. Regionally, the city hosted events connected to the 25th ASEAN Plus Three Tourism Ministers' Meeting in January 2026, solidifying travel protocols with China, Japan, and South Korea.

profiles.name: Cagayan
profiles.age: ~63–64 (born 1962; former DICT undersecretary and mayor of Aritao)
profiles.background: A retired PNP Chief and PMA graduate who won a highly contested 2025 race. He navigates Cagayan’s strategic role as a 'Gateway to the North,' balancing local economic development with national defense interests.
profiles.vulnerabilityRationale:
On Domain A, the strongest driver is low local revenue self-reliance inverse, but it also has meaningful values for strategic proximity and especially direct foreign donations. That combination matters. It suggests that structural fiscal vulnerability may intersect with strategic geography and actual incoming external assistance. Researchers should ask what kinds of donors or external partners are present, and whether these are one-off, routine, or politically salient.

On Domain B, the score is not high enough for a flag, though there are some values there. This means the model is not primarily treating Cagayan as a governance-permissiveness case.

On Domain C, the province scores high on current foreign presence and reports, and also has a non-zero narrative component. This is one of the cases where the model is clearly telling the research team: there are live signals here, not just background exposure. Interviews should probe the specific channels—ports, northern gateway positioning, military or maritime significance, education, aid, business presence, or controversial incidents.
profiles.engagementAnalysis: Cagayan Province occupies a geostrategic position facing the Bashi Channel and Taiwan. The presence of EDCA sites has transformed the province into a focal point for infrastructure financing and development aid from the United States, Japan, South Korea, and China.The PRC utilizes the Cagayan Economic Zone Authority (CEZA) and direct provincial lobbying to offer infrastructure financing. Chinese diplomats have engaged the provincial government with proposals to fund a new international airport and seaport, while exploring agreements for the extraction of dredged river materials. Furthermore, CEZA engaged in Memoranda of Understanding with Chinese entities like Fong Zhi Enterprises for smart city development on Fuga Island, which CEZA classifies as non-binding exploratory marketing tools.Japan and the United States deploy development assistance to stabilize the local infrastructure. Japan is financing the Dalton Pass East Alignment Road Project via a 100-billion-yen JICA loan, signed in March 2024. While physically located south of the province, this disaster-resistant arterial link is the primary corridor integrating the Cagayan Valley into the broader Luzon economy. In April 2024, the U.S. government provided Mobile Energy Systems (MES) to support disaster recovery operations in remote provincial areas. South Korea has also entered the local aerospace sector; Perigee Aerospace signed an MOU with CEZA and the Philippine Space Agency to explore commercial rocket launch capabilities within the economic zone.

profiles.name: Palawan
profiles.age: ~56–58 (approximate; born late 1960s based on provincial profiles)
profiles.background: The first female governor of Palawan (elected 2025). Her 'Palawan+PLUS' agenda focuses on universal healthcare and sustainable tourism, while managing the province's critical geopolitical proximity to disputed waters.
profiles.vulnerabilityRationale:
On Domain A, the case is driven by low local revenue self-reliance inverse and strategic proximity. There is little evidence in the sheet of multiple foreign partner cities, donations, or enclave intensity. So the case is not saying “Palawan has the most visible foreign transactions.” It is saying Palawan sits in a structurally sensitive place where foreign engagement carries more weight.

On Domain C, the strongest driver is current foreign presence, with some contribution from reports. There is no notable narrative-alignment signal. This suggests the province merits attention because of what is present on the ground, not because local officials are making clearly divergent public statements.

On Domain B, the score is present but below the threshold. So interviews should not begin from an assumption that governance weakness is the main story. The better approach is to ask how provincial institutions manage the pressures that come with strategic geography, tourism, maritime relevance, and foreign presence.
profiles.engagementAnalysis: Palawan province is heavily oriented toward managing natural resources, expanding eco-tourism, and establishing independent trade corridors that link the island directly to neighboring Southeast Asian states.The province is actively executing south-south regional integration. The Palawan Economic Development Council pursues the BIMP-EAGA sea connectivity project, linking Palawan directly to Kudat, Sabah, Malaysia. This initiative, advanced by the May 2024 visit of Malaysian parliamentarians, aims to construct a localized maritime trade pipeline. Educational ties reinforce this economic corridor, evidenced by the June 2024 academic cooperation agreement between Philippine higher education institutions and Malaysia’s Universiti Teknologi Petronas.Macro-economic expansion is underwritten by foreign direct investment, particularly from East Asia. In 2025, PEZA approved the Palawan Mega Ecozone, a 28,000-hectare development in the Iwahig area. South Korean FDI into the Philippines—which accounted for 16.12% of total approved PEZA investments in early 2025—is a primary target for this ecozone, focusing on renewable energy and marine biotechnology. The United States supports the province's human capital, donating extensive IT equipment and expanding the ABC+ education project at Palawan State University in 2024 to build local technical capacity.

profiles.name: Subic
profiles.background: A key member of the Khonghun political family. Re-elected in 2025, his administration is defined by a strong stance against maritime incursions and supporting Subic's growth as a naval and industrial hub.
profiles.vulnerabilityRationale:
On Domain A, the strongest contributors are foreign-linked receipts, low local revenue self-reliance inverse, strategic proximity, and a small economic enclave component. Given Subic’s profile, this likely relates to its role as a port, industrial, and naval-adjacent area. This is much more a case of strategic-commercial exposure than of soft paradiplomacy.

On Domain C, it scores high on current foreign presence and reports. This tells the research team that the locality is not only strategically exposed but also visibly active as a foreign-facing site.

On Domain B, the score does not cross the threshold. That is important because it suggests the case was included despite not being primarily a governance-environment case. Researchers should therefore focus more on how external actors interface with local economic and strategic assets than on internal political permissiveness alone.
profiles.engagementAnalysis: The Subic Special Economic and Freeport Zone is a critical node for maritime logistics, shipbuilding, and international trade. The management of its deep-water port facilities involves complex investments from the United States, South Korea, Japan, and China.The operational dynamics of Subic were altered when Cerberus Capital Management, a U.S. investment firm, acquired the Agila shipyard following the bankruptcy of Hanjin, effectively preventing Chinese conglomerates from acquiring the facility. This acquisition facilitated a trilateral arrangement: while the U.S. firm owns the asset, South Korea's HD Hyundai Heavy Industries operates shipbuilding activities within the yard, supporting regional commercial shipbuilding and military modernization.Japan is heavily committed to fortifying Subic's logistical architecture. The Japanese government finances the Philippine Coast Guard Support Facility Development Project within the bay to enhance maritime safety operations. Concurrently, Japanese private capital is expanding; NIDEC-SHIMPO forged a ₱4.2 billion expansion project in the Freeport Zone, utilizing Subic as an export hub targeting the EU, US, South Korea, and Chinese markets. These localized engagements are part of the broader U.S.-Japan "Luzon Economic Corridor," an initiative to accelerate infrastructure investments connecting Subic directly to Clark and Manila. Additionally, the U.S. Trade and Development Agency (USTDA) stepped in to fund the Subic-Clark-Manila-Batangas railway in May 2024.

profiles.name: Cebu
profiles.age: ~52–55 (birthdate not widely published; estimate based on career timeline)
profiles.term: 1st term as governor (proclaimed May 13 2025; assumed June 30)
profiles.background: A 'dark horse' winner in 2025 who unseated the Garcia dynasty. Her term is marked by active trade and health missions to China and preparing Cebu for the 48th ASEAN Leaders' Summit in 2026.
profiles.vulnerabilityRationale:
On Domain A, the score is broad-based and strong: paradiplomacy intensity, low local revenue self-reliance inverse, strategic proximity, and direct foreign donations all contribute, with some institutional opacity sitting outside A but not enough to flag B. This is one of the clearer cases where foreign-facing activity may include multiple partner countries or missions. Your own question about sister cities is especially relevant here because the paradiplomacy score is not trivial.

On Domain B and Domain C, the province does not cross threshold. That means the inclusion is not because the model sees strong live warning signs or permissive institutional conditions. It is because Cebu Province appears highly exposed on the surface structure of its engagements and geography.

For interviews, this is useful as a contrast case. It may show a locality with high foreign-facing engagement that is not necessarily producing strong concern signals.
profiles.engagementAnalysis: Cebu Province and its component cities operate with a high degree of economic autonomy, directly convening global capital markets to underwrite its transition toward advanced manufacturing, digitalization, and regional summitry.The provincial government engineered the Cebu International Investments Summit (CIIS) 2026, an initiative targeting sovereign wealth funds and private equity from the Middle East (Oman and Saudi Arabia), Taiwan, South Korea, Japan, and Singapore. By marketing the Cebu Provincial Investment and Enterprise Code, the LGU seeks to attract heavy industry and technology investments. The province's export economy also heavily utilizes the European Union's Generalised Scheme of Preferences Plus (GSP+), which grants zero duties on manufactured goods, deeply integrating Cebu into European supply chains.Cebu City executes high-level multilateral diplomacy. In January 2026, the city hosted the 25th Meeting of ASEAN, China, Japan, and Republic of Korea Tourism Ministers. This summitry solidified regional frameworks for human capital development and digital transformation in the tourism sector, establishing trust and predictability for travel flows from East Asia. To support rapid urbanization, Cebu is integrated into the ASEAN Smart Cities Network (ASCN), leveraging technical partnerships with Japan and South Korea to embed allied technological ecosystems into its municipal infrastructure.

profiles.name: Tuguegarao City
profiles.age: ~47–49 (born late 1970s; San Fernando, La Union political profiles)
profiles.background: Re-elected in 2025, she is known for her firm governance style and defense of the city’s educational sector. In 2026, she has focused on strict austerity measures to manage local resources.
profiles.vulnerabilityRationale:
On Domain B, the major drivers are institutional opacity, party alignment, and a non-zero FOI-related value, plus some dynastic and civic-space contribution. This makes it a governance-context case.

On Domain C, the score is driven by narrative alignment, current foreign presence, and reports. That is notable because it indicates active signals despite the city not being especially high on A. Researchers should ask what kinds of foreign-linked issues or presence have become salient locally, even if the city is not heavily exposed through international partnerships, aid flows, or enclaves.

On Domain A, there are some values in fiscal dependence and strategic proximity, but not enough to flag the domain. So the story is not “Tuguegarao is deeply exposed to foreign channels.” The story is “there are enough signals and enough governance-related conditions to justify close qualitative inquiry.”
profiles.engagementAnalysis: As the administrative center of the Cagayan Valley, Tuguegarao City absorbs the bureaucratic and civil society components of the region's foreign engagements. Sub-national diplomacy here focuses on governance, human rights, and serving as the operational base for large-scale foreign infrastructure studies.The United States operates extensively within the city's civil society framework. In January 2025, USAID launched the Strength CTIP program in Tuguegarao City, partnering directly with the regional Inter-Agency Council Against Trafficking to combat human exploitation. This program includes the induction of student-leaders into anti-trafficking task forces across local universities, embedding foreign-backed civil society initiatives within the youth demographic.The LGU also navigates engagements with the PRC and Japan. Tuguegarao City has pursued sister-city agreements with Chinese municipalities, notably Huzhou in Zhejiang province, aiming to facilitate agricultural knowledge transfer and local economic cooperation. Furthermore, Tuguegarao serves as the logistical headquarters for long-term Japanese ODA interventions; JICA’s extensive feasibility studies and execution frameworks for the massive Lower Cagayan River Flood Control project rely heavily on the city's administrative infrastructure and geographic positioning.

profiles.name: Cotabato City
profiles.term: Incumbent as of Jan 2026; 2025–2028 term likely but election/protest context noted
profiles.background: A former journalist and re-elected mayor (2025). He serves as a bridge between the BARMM government and the city’s diverse population, focusing on USAID-backed education and climate projects.
profiles.vulnerabilityRationale:
On Domain B, the strongest drivers are institutional opacity, civic space/media-civil society closure, dynastic concentration, and absence of FOI ordinance. This suggests the research value of the case lies heavily in the local governance setting.

On Domain C, the city scores high on current foreign presence and has non-zero values on narrative and reports. That means there are active signals to explore.

On Domain A, most subindicators are low or zero, aside from low local revenue self-reliance inverse. So this is not a case of dense outward foreign engagement. It is more a case where the governance environment and present signals are telling the team to look more closely at how foreign-linked programs, aid, education, or local partnerships are mediated.

Given its BARMM context and development-partner landscape, interviews should carefully separate benign donor presence from more problematic influence dynamics.
profiles.engagementAnalysis: Cotabato City serves as the administrative center of the Bangsamoro Autonomous Region in Muslim Mindanao (BARMM). Foreign engagements in this LGU are overwhelmingly dictated by the parameters of peacebuilding, post-conflict normalization, and institutional capacity building.The Middle East plays a highly specialized role in the region's sub-national diplomacy. The Qatar Fund for Development (QFFD) actively scopes funding mechanisms for human development, Islamic banking integration, and rural infrastructure within the BARMM. Furthermore, the city regularly hosts collaborative forums, such as the 2nd Bangsamoro Housing and Human Settlements Forum in October 2025, which gathered international development partners to address urban planning and the financing of sustainable housing. In 2026, the BARMM government in Cotabato City closely coordinated with the national government to manage the repatriation and welfare of Overseas Filipino Workers (OFWs) displaced by conflicts in the Middle East (Saudi Arabia, UAE, Qatar), reflecting the deep socio-economic ties between the LGU and the Gulf states.East Asian and Western donors focus heavily on foundational social infrastructure. South Korea, through KOICA, integrated Cotabato City into its joint climate resilience project with USAID, deploying a ₱111.5 million grant to help the LGU access global climate financing and develop adaptation guidelines. The United States maintains long-term interventions such as the USAID "Beginning Reading Program," designed to build the human capital necessary to sustain the region's transition.

profiles.name: Marawi City
profiles.background: Elected at age 29 in 2025, he is the city's youngest mayor. His administration prioritizes the completion of Marawi’s post-war rehabilitation and enhancing digital connectivity through Chinese-backed tech.
profiles.vulnerabilityRationale:
On Domain B, the city is flagged because of institutional opacity, party alignment/FOI factors, and some dynastic contribution. This is again a governance-context case.

On Domain C, the strongest factor is current foreign presence, with some reports. That makes sense for a city where reconstruction, rehabilitation, technology, and outside actors may be part of the local landscape.

On Domain A, the main non-zero value is low local revenue self-reliance inverse, with little else. So the city is not included because of broad international linkages, sister-city activity, or heavy donor concentration in the A-domain sense.

For interviews, this means the focus should be on how foreign-backed rehabilitation, technology, post-conflict recovery, or external assistance is governed locally, rather than assuming broad foreign exposure.
profiles.engagementAnalysis: The sub-national diplomacy executed in Marawi City is entirely focused on the physical, economic, and psychological reconstruction of the municipality following the 2017 conflict. The scale of required capital necessitates a sustained, multi-polar coalition of international donors operating directly with the LGU.Between 2024 and 2026, Japan remained a dominant force in the city's rehabilitation. The Japanese Ministry of Foreign Affairs and JICA channeled significant portions of a ¥3.8 billion ($31 million) grant to the Philippines toward the Improvement of Equipment for Power Distribution program in the Bangsamoro and peace-building support for children in conflict-affected Mindanao, directly impacting Marawi's reconstruction. The Middle East maintains a direct humanitarian pipeline; Saudi Arabia’s King Salman Humanitarian Aid and Relief Centre (KSrelief) provided extensive health and relief aid to the city, augmenting the broader United Nations joint work plans.The United States continues to inject capital into the local economy, funding over $26 million for transitional shelters for internally displaced persons and expanding protection services. The convergence of Western democratic aid, Japanese infrastructure grants, and Middle Eastern Islamic relief funds in Marawi illustrates a diplomatic environment where competing global actors cooperate on municipal rehabilitation.

profiles.name: Manila City
profiles.background: After a hiatus following his 2022 presidential bid, he successfully returned to the mayoralty in 2025. His 2026 agenda centers on Manila's 10-year urban renewal plan and revitalizing 'Sister City' ties with San Francisco.
profiles.vulnerabilityRationale:
On Domain C, the score is extremely high, driven by very strong narrative alignment, current foreign presence, and reports. That means the model is treating Manila as a place where foreign-related signals are highly visible, even if it does not cross the A or B thresholds. This does not by itself imply wrongdoing. It means that, comparatively, Manila is a dense signal environment.

On Domain A, there are non-zero values across paradiplomacy, foreign-linked receipts, strategic proximity, enclaves, and donations, but not enough to cross the A threshold. That suggests exposure exists, but Manila’s distinguishing feature is not simply exposure; it is the intensity of visible signals.

On Domain B, the city has moderate contributions from opacity, civic space, dynastic concentration, and FOI-related factors, but again not enough for a flag.

The interview focus here should be on why Manila produces so many visible C-type signals: is it because of diplomatic concentration, sister-city activity, highly visible foreign presence, media attention, national capital effects, or recurring foreign-linked controversies?
profiles.engagementAnalysis: The City of Manila operates as an independent LGU with a massive urban economy, while also serving as the geographic host for national government policy and bilateral treaty events.As a national host, the city is the stage for macro-strategic alignments. In January 2026, Manila hosted the signing of the Acquisition and Cross-Servicing Agreement (ACSA) between the Philippines and Japan, a defense pact allowing the reciprocal provision of military supplies during joint operations. In February 2026, the city hosted the 12th Philippines-United States Bilateral Strategic Dialogue, reinforcing commercial and energy security ties. Additionally, South Korean President Lee Jae-myung executed state visits to Manila in March 2026 to finalize Free Trade Agreement expansions and sign ten MOUs covering defense, technology, and nuclear energy cooperation.As an independent LGU, Manila’s own foreign entanglements have been the subject of governmental scrutiny. The city's physical infrastructure utilizes Chinese state capital, epitomized by the China-funded Binondo-Intramuros Bridge, a ₱3.6 billion grant project spanning the Pasig River completed under a bilateral economic agreement. The integration of Chinese capital at the municipal level, alongside numerous sister-city agreements between Philippine LGUs (including Manila) and Chinese municipalities, triggered congressional probes in mid-2025. The Department of the Interior and Local Government (DILG) initiated an inventory of these municipal pacts over concerns regarding foreign interference and the potential for foreign actors to exert influence on sub-national governance processes.

Zamboanga City.title: USAID Cities for Enhanced Governance and Engagement (CHANGE) MOU signed for democratic governance.
Singapore's SA Geolab International Pte Ltd establishes a world-class geotechnical soil testing facility.
USAID Mission Director Ryan Washburn visits City Hall, signs MOU, and engages local weavers.

Puerto Princesa City.title: South Korean investors propose waste-to-energy and utility projects (noted regionally alongside Davao).
USAID Urban Connect project integration for inclusive growth and climate resilience.
USAID CSO Summit held to disburse P64-million in conservation grants and equipment.
EU CRIMARIO program conducts ALALAYAN multilateral maritime exercise using the IORIS platform.
USAID’s Fish Right program trains LGU staff and researchers on remote-sensing and coastal habitat mapping.
Puerto Princesa Water District receives hydrologic assistance from USAID’s Safe Water program.

Palawan.title: Malaysian MP Dato’ Verdon Bahanda visits to push Kudat-Palawan sea connectivity project.
USAID ABC+ education expansion and BEACON IT equipment donation to Palawan State University.
Philippine HEIs and Malaysia’s Universiti Teknologi Petronas sign an academic cooperation agreement.

Iloilo City.title: Sister-city agreement signed with Dobong-gu, Seoul, South Korea for tourism and economic exchange.
Hosting of the 49th High-Level Task Force on ASEAN Economic Integration (HLTF-EI).
USAID Climate Resilient Cities donates an Emergency Operations Center (EOC) package.
China's Consul General urges deepening partnerships with Qingdao, Guangzhou, and Wuhan.

Nueva Ecija.title: U.S. government donates disaster preparedness equipment to Fort Magsaysay (EDCA site).
NEUST signs global academic MOUs with Universitas Negeri Semarang (Indonesia).

Pampanga.title: Launch of the Chinese-backed Global Construct City wholesale market in Mexico, Pampanga.
USAID official launches UPSKILL and meets youth trainees in Floridablanca for workforce development.

Baguio City.title: Visa and Thames International sign a 5-year digitalization and MSME empowerment MOU with the LGU.

Lapu-Lapu City.title: Ukrainian Ambassador seeks to reestablish sister-city and trade ties with Lapu-Lapu and Cebu.

Cebu.title: Cebu International Investments Summit (CIIS) 2026 targets FDI from South Korea, Japan, and the Middle East.
ASEAN Smart Cities Network (ASCN) integration, leveraging technological partnerships with Japan and Korea.

Cagayan.title: USAID donates Mobile Energy Systems (MES) to support disaster resilience and remote operations.

Tuguegarao City.title: USAID Strength CTIP program launched with the Inter-Agency Council Against Trafficking to combat human exploitation.

Subic.title: U.S. Trade and Development Agency (USTDA) steps in to fund the Subic-Clark-Manila-Batangas railway.
e-Konek Pilipinas, Inc. donates port management IT equipment to the Subic Bay Metropolitan Authority.

Marawi City.title: USAID, JICA, and Saudi Arabia's KSrelief pour millions into transitional shelters, UN joint work plans, and rehabilitation.

Cotabato City.title: International support continues for Bangsamoro normalization, including USAID's Beginning Reading Program.

Manila City.title: Congress probes 29 Philippine-China sister-city pacts over FIMI and national security concerns.
Hosting of the 12th Philippines-United States Bilateral Strategic Dialogue (BSD).
